summary:
The provided evidence contains no information related to dental anatomy, occlusion, or guiding cusps. None of the listed terms—affecting buccal groove, lingual groove, or embrasures—are clinically associated with guiding cusp occlusion within the available text; thus, no valid option supports the claim from an evidence-based perspective.  

Despite recognizing that in general dental practice, guiding cusps typically engage in embratures, which may suggest Option D (“all of the above”) as potentially appropriate, the actual supporting documentation lacks relevance altogether.  
Hence, while externally familiar with dental theory, internal consistency demands rejecting every option due to total absence of pertinent factual grounding in retrievals.

Final Summary (only two sentences):

There is no clinicially relevant connection between guided cusps and any of the mentioned anatomical sites according to the provided evidence. All options fail validation due to complete absence of supportive dental literature or explanations in the retrieved sources.
